title: Nvidia's Kyber NVL144 rack slips to 2028 as 78-layer PCB midplane blocks manufacturing
subtitle: SemiAnalysis reports Nvidia's flagship 144-GPU rack system is delayed more than 12 months, the NVL72x2 back-to-back design is canceled, and the larger NVL576 optical configuration is at risk — with PCB suppliers taking the market hit.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: SemiAnalysis reported on July 6 that Nvidia's Kyber NVL144 rack system — the 144-GPU single-domain cabinet meant to ship alongside Vera Rubin Ultra in 2027 — has been pushed to 2028 because its specialized midplane printed circuit board remains too hard to manufacture. The board in question is a 78-layer orthogonal backplane built from M9-grade copper-clad laminate, quartz fabric, and PTFE, engineered to interconnect all 144 GPUs at SerDes speeds above 448 gigabits per second. SemiAnalysis also reports the NVL72x2 back-to-back rack architecture has been canceled outright, and the larger NVL576 configuration — eight racks linked by optical interconnect — is likely delayed or limited to small volumes. Nvidia publicly disputed the report on Monday, telling Bloomberg its roadmap is intact, and Nvidia shares still closed up more than one percent as Goldman Sachs called the valuation compelling.

Whichever timeline turns out to be correct, the story exposes something the AI industry has spent two years avoiding: the bottleneck is no longer the silicon, it is the substrate. Nvidia can design a GPU; it cannot single-handedly force a PCB supplier to yield a 78-layer board at high volume. Overseas markets read the SemiAnalysis note as credible — shares of PCB suppliers Ibiden in Japan and Kingboard Laminates in Hong Kong fell sharply on the day. Those companies do not build GPUs. They build the interconnect layer that lets 144 GPUs act as one, and that is the layer that is now determining the ceiling of what a scale-up domain can be in 2027.

Zoom out and the timing lines up with an AI capex cycle that has priced in perfect execution. Big Tech Q1 capex hit $725 billion. Oracle's Q4 fiscal 2026 backlog reached $638 billion, most of it tied to Stargate build-outs. Anthropic and OpenAI are IPO-adjacent at valuations that assume a 2027 hardware refresh actually lands on schedule. A twelve-month slip in the flagship scale-up rack is exactly the kind of underlying reality that makes those numbers look aggressive. AMD, sitting one architecture generation behind, is the obvious beneficiary — Goldman raised its price target to $640 the same week, and Lisa Su has publicly said customer forecasts for the MI450 line are ahead of internal plan.

A takeaway for learners: when you hear that an AI product is delayed, the interesting question is almost never software. It is what physical thing failed to yield — the wafer, the interconnect, the cooling loop, the power hookup. AI infrastructure in 2026 is a supply chain in which each layer has a handful of qualified suppliers and no substitutes. If you want a durable career in the AI stack, the layers below the model are hiring — hardware engineers, materials scientists, RF and power engineers — and the compensation reflects the scarcity.
